title: Making a leather cord organiser
subtitle: Saddle-stitch a small veg-tan leather pouch with slots to hold and organise charging cables and earphones on a desk
excerpt: How to make a leather cord organiser from vegetable-tanned leather. Covers cutting the body and slot strips, skiving the edges, punching stitch holes, saddle-stitching the slots to the body, and finishing the edges with a burnisher.
sourceNotes: Traditional leatherwork saddle-stitch and hand-sewing technique. Al Stohlman, The Art of Hand Sewing Leather (referenced principles, not reproduced). UK leatherworking trade practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: Leather tanned using plant-based tannins from bark, leaves, and gallnuts rather than chromium salts. Veg-tan is stiffer and firmer than chrome-tanned leather; it holds tooled patterns and acquires a patina with use. It is the standard material for leatherwork projects that are hand-stitched, tooled, or moulded (the material responds predictably to cutting, skiving, punching, and burnishing. Available in natural (cream-tan), brown, and black. Thickness for cord organisers and small accessories: 1.2 to 1.5 mm.

content.text: A leather cord organiser is a flat

piece with raised slots that hold cables upright) the slots are stitched-down leather tabs. The result sits on a desk or in a bag and keeps charging cables and earphone leads separated. The technique is cutting, skiving the slot tabs, punching stitch holes, and saddle-stitching.
Cut the body and slot tabs

Cut the body from 1.5 mm veg-tan: 150 mm × 90 mm. Cut four or five slot tabs from the same leather: each tab 35 mm × 20 mm. Mark the slot positions on the body in pencil (equally spaced across the width, 30 mm from the top edge. The tab positions should leave 15 mm margins from each body edge.

Skive the bottom 5 mm of each tab on the flesh side) taper the edge thin with the leatherwork knife at a shallow angle so the tab base is 0.5 mm thick at the edge. This allows the tab to fold over neatly without a ridge. Bevel all four edges of each tab with the edge beveller and burnish the top and side edges smooth with the burnisher and water.

Mark a 3 mm stitch line along the bottom of each tab position on the body. Punch stitch holes through both the tab base and the body together using a pricking iron at 3 mm spacing. Saddle-stitch through both layers with two needles and waxed linen thread, working each hole with a locked stitch. When the row is complete, backstitch the last three holes and trim the thread ends close.

Bevel all four body edges with the edge beveller. Wet the edges slightly with a finger dipped in water. Burnish all edges firmly with the burnisher: working back and forth until the fibres compress and the edge slicks to a smooth, firm finish. The burnished edge is the mark of a finished leatherwork piece.
